food_subgroup: Hot milk-based tea beverage — matcha powder whisked with steamed whole milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for matcha latte. Closest parent concept: green tea beverage with milk (FOODON:03301737 — tea beverage). No specific matcha latte entry available in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_description: Matcha powder with steamed whole milk (matcha latte) — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy for green tea-based milk beverage
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BVT is the closest defensible FoodEx2 code for a green tea-based beverage; no exact matcha latte code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for matcha latte as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard café serving of matcha latte is approximately 300ml (one cup), comprising ~4g ceremonial or culinary grade matcha powder whisked with ~250ml steamed whole milk, optionally sweetened with ~10g honey, yielding approximately 216 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Whole Milk (FDC ID 1097512) combined with matcha powder nutrient profile derived from USDA FoodData Central green tea powder entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of ~83.3% whole milk (250g) and ~1.3% matcha powder (4g) in a 300g serving, calculated from USDA FoodData Central whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) and USDA FoodData Central green tea / matcha powder entries. The Japan MEXT 8th Edition Standard Tables of Food Composition is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; matcha powder micronutrient contributions were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central green tea entries and HPB Singapore nutrient data where available. Values represent the blended beverage per 100g of total serving weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Milk and Dairy Products — University of Sydney GI Database; lactose GI reference and milk GI analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for matcha latte as a composite beverage. GI estimated at ~30 using the University of Sydney GI Database reference for whole milk (GI ~27–34, lactose GI ~46 moderated by milk fat and protein matrix). Matcha powder contributes negligible carbohydrate (~0.5g per 4g dose) and EGCG may exert modest alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity, further moderating postprandial glucose response. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (30 × 21g) / 100 = 6.3, rounded to 6.0. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~156mg per 300ml serving); EGCG may modestly improve endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: At ~52mg sodium per 100g (~156mg per 300ml cup), this beverage is very low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management; EGCG from matcha has been associated with modest systolic blood pressure reduction (~3mmHg) in meta-analyses of green tea consumption at ≥3 cups/day, though a single cup provides sub-therapeutic EGCG levels (~70mg vs. ~400mg therapeutic dose).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load (GL 6 per serving); EGCG alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity; lactose as primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of ~30 and GL of ~6 per 300ml serving, this matcha latte is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without honey; the milk lactose (GI ~46) is moderated by the milk fat and protein matrix, and EGCG from matcha may provide modest postprandial glucose attenuation via alpha-glucosidase inhibition — omitting the optional honey (~10g) reduces GL by approximately 4 units.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from whole milk (~7.2g per serving); EGCG antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 300ml serving provides ~7.2g saturated fat from whole milk (approximately 36% of the 20g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet), which warrants moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease; however, the very low sodium content (~156mg), EGCG-mediated LDL oxidation inhibition, and L-theanine stress-response modulation offer partial cardiovascular benefit — substituting low-fat or plant-based milk would substantially reduce saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EGCG anti-inflammatory via NF-κB pathway inhibition; calcium from milk supports bone health in corticosteroid-treated patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: EGCG from matcha inhibits NF-κB inflammatory signalling and L-theanine may reduce cortisol-mediated stress inflammation; the ~336mg calcium per 300ml serving (28% of RDI) is clinically relevant for RA patients on corticosteroids who face elevated osteoporosis risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha Latte (Singapore Specialty Café Style) is a hot milk-based beverage combining ~4g of stone-ground Japanese matcha powder (Camellia sinensis whole leaf, ceremonial or culinary grade) whisked with ~250ml of steamed whole milk, optionally sweetened with ~10g honey, served in a ~300ml cup. Matcha powder is produced by shade-growing green tea leaves for 3–4 weeks before harvest to boost chlorophyll and L-theanine content, then stone-grinding the dried leaves into a fine powder — unlike steeped green tea, matcha involves consuming the entire leaf, yielding significantly higher polyphenol, L-theanine (~30mg per 4g dose), and caffeine (~40mg per 4g dose) concentrations. The beverage has a smooth, creamy texture with a characteristic umami-vegetal bitterness balanced by the natural sweetness of whole milk lactose. Per 300ml serving: approximately 216 kcal, 10.5g protein, 11.4g fat (7.2g saturated), 21g carbohydrate, 156mg sodium. GI ~30 (low), GL ~6 (low). Key bioactive compounds per serving: ~70mg EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), ~40mg caffeine, ~30mg L-theanine — note that milk casein may reduce EGCG bioavailability by 10–25%. Therapeutic EGCG dose (~400mg/day) requires approximately 6 cups. Calcium content ~336mg per serving (28% RDI). Suitable for vegetarians, halal, gluten-free. Singapore specialty café context: widely available at specialty cafés, Starbucks Singapore, and Japanese dessert cafés since ~2012. Nutritional data derived from USDA FoodData Central whole milk and green tea powder entries.
common_user_queries: matcha latte vs coffee health benefits
is matcha latte good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central whole milk (FDC ID 1097512) — an approved primary source — with matcha powder micronutrient contributions cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central green tea powder entries. The composite per-100g profile represents a weighted blend of the two primary ingredients at known proportions (250g whole milk + 4g matcha powder in 300g total serving). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database whole milk reference (GI ~27–34), which is the approved GI source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a whole-milk matcha beverage.
notes: 1. COMPOSITE BEVERAGE: Per-100g values represent a weighted blend of ~83.3% whole milk (250g per 300ml serving) and ~1.3% matcha powder (4g per 300ml serving), with the remainder being water used in whisking. Values calculated from USDA FoodData Central approved sources. 2. DIETARY FIBRE: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed as effectively zero for the liquid beverage fraction; matcha powder contributes trace fibre (~0.1g per 4g dose) which rounds to 0.0 per 100g of the blended beverage. This is a confirmed near-zero value, not a data gap. 3. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally occurring ruminant trans fats in whole milk are present at trace levels (<0.1g per 100g) and reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central. 4. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: monounsaturated_fat_g (1.1g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central whole milk fatty acid profile proportionally scaled to the composite beverage. 5. WATER: water_g set to 85.0g per 100g — estimated from the high liquid content of the beverage (whole milk ~87.7% water, composite beverage approximately 85% water after accounting for matcha solids). 6. OPTIONAL HONEY: The optional honey (~10g) is not included in the per-100g values; if included, carbohydrate_g increases by approximately 2.7g per 100g of the 300ml serving, raising GL by ~4 units. 7. SOURCE NOTE: The Japan MEXT 8th Edition Standard Tables of Food Composition cited in the original input record is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2. All values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. 8. EGCG BIOAVAILABILITY: Milk casein may reduce EGCG bioavailability by 10–25%; this does not affect the nutritional per-100g values but is clinically relevant for polyphenol benefit claims. 9. FOODEX2 CODE A0BVT: Used as closest-parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for matcha latte exists as of 2026-04.
